hero_title: Evolution of Operating Systems – from the first computers to the present
hero_subtitle: This website presents the evolution of operating systems, their role in computer operation, and the differences between the main types of systems used throughout history.
learning_title: What you'll learn from this site
learning_intro: This educational material covers the main aspects related to operating systems:
learning_what_desc: The definition and main components of an operating system, the basic software that controls a computer.
learning_role_desc: Essential functions: memory management, processes, files, and input/output devices.
learning_evolution_desc: History of operating systems from the 1950s to the present, with the main stages and innovations.
learning_diff_desc: Comparison between past and contemporary operating systems: interfaces, performance, security.
learning_examples_desc: Presentation of the main operating systems: Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS and others.
learning_windows_desc: Visually explore all Windows versions from 95 to 11, with interactive simulations of the original interfaces.
timeline_1950s_desc: The first OSs were developed for mainframe computers. They executed one task at a time. Users had to use punched cards for input.
timeline_1970s_desc: Unix brought a revolution through portability and modular design. It allowed multiple users to work simultaneously on the same computer. The C language made Unix spread easily.
timeline_1980s_desc: Microsoft DOS dominated personal computers. Apple Macintosh introduced the graphical user interface (GUI). The personal computer era began to replace mainframes.
timeline_1990s_desc: Windows 3.0 made GUI accessible to the masses. Linux emerged as an open-source alternative. The Internet began to become dominant. Windows 95 marked a major change in OS interface.
timeline_2000s_systems: Windows XP (2001), Mac OS X (2001), Android (2008), iOS (2007)
timeline_2000s_desc: Emergence of mobile operating systems. Cloud computing began to develop. Windows XP series was extremely popular. Linux consolidated on servers.
timeline_2010s_desc: Operating systems become smarter with AI. Hybrid computers with no boundaries between devices. Increasing importance of security and privacy. Open-source becomes mainstream.
os_windows_desc: The most used OS on desktops. Developed by Microsoft. Dominates the personal computer market with approximately 75% market share.
os_macos_desc: Apple's computer operating system. Known for stability and elegant design. Unix-based, very popular among creatives and developers.
os_linux_desc: The free, open-source operating system. Dominates web servers with over 96% market share. Very flexible and customizable.
os_android_desc: OS for mobile devices. Developed by Google. The most used OS globally with over 70% of the smartphone market.
os_ios_desc: OS exclusively for iPhone and iPad. Developed by Apple. Known for security and closed ecosystem.
os_chromeos_desc: The Chromebook operating system. Lightweight, fast and secure. Linux-based, focused on cloud computing.
about_subtitle: Discover the story behind this website
history_hero_subtitle: From punched cards to cloud and artificial intelligence – a journey through the evolution of technology
comparison_subtitle: Detailed analysis of different current operating systems
glossary_subtitle: Over 50 technical terms explained in detail with practical examples
windows_subtitle: Explore the history of the operating system that changed the world
windows_intro_desc: Discover the visual and functional evolution of the most popular desktop operating system. Each version brought innovations that defined how we interact with technology.
dyk_win95: Windows 95 was the first Microsoft operating system with the Start button and taskbar. It was launched on August 24, 1995 and sold 7 million copies in the first 5 weeks!
dyk_win98: Windows 98 was the first Windows with native support for USB and DVD! It also introduced Windows Update for automatic updates via the internet.
dyk_winxp: Windows XP was the longest-lived Windows, being supported for 13 years (2001-2014). The "Bliss" wallpaper is the most viewed photograph in history!
dyk_vista: Windows Vista introduced the Aero Glass interface with transparency effects and was the first Windows with User Account Control (UAC) for enhanced security.
dyk_win7: Windows 7 is considered by many to be the best Windows ever. It introduced Snap for window arrangement and sold 100 million licenses in the first 6 months!
dyk_win8: Windows 8 introduced the Metro interface (Modern UI) optimized for touch and removed the classic Start button, which caused major controversies.
dyk_win10: Windows 10 was offered free for the first year! It's the first Windows "as a Service" with continuous updates. Cortana, the voice assistant, debuted here.
dyk_win11: Windows 11 brought a completely new design with rounded corners and centered taskbar. It's the first Windows to natively support Android apps!

ch4_title: Chapter 4: The PC and GUI Era
ch4_subtitle: Chapter 4: The PC and GUI Era
ch5_lead: Linus Torvalds and the free software revolution
ch5_context_body: Linux started as a free kernel, developed by Linus Torvalds and supported by a global developer community.
ch5_feature_open: Open source code — anyone can study and modify the code.
ch5_feature_community: Global community — collaborative development and shared reviews.
ch5_feature_security: Stability and security — used on servers and critical systems.
ch5_usage_body: Linux is used on servers, embedded devices, supercomputers, and in many distributions as a daily driver for developers.
ch5_media_tux: Tux — the unofficial Linux mascot (source: Wikimedia Commons)
ch5_media_linus: Linus Torvalds — initiator of the Linux kernel (source: Wikimedia Commons)
ch5_nav_prev: ← Back to the PC and GUI Era
ch6_lead: Windows, macOS, Linux, mobile, cloud and AI
ch6_context_body: In the modern era, operating systems run on diverse devices: PCs, phones, servers, and cloud infrastructure. The demand for performance, security, and interoperability has grown significantly.
ch6_feature_multitask: Advanced multitasking — efficient process scheduling and resource management.
ch6_feature_cloud: Cloud and AI support — integration with external services and intelligent features.
ch6_feature_security: Security and constant updates — patches, sandboxing, and process isolation.
ch6_examples_body: Among the most widespread are Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS, each optimized for different use cases.
ch6_notes_body: The current focus is on security, user experience, and interoperability across devices, while regular updates keep systems safer and more performant.
ch6_media_servers: Servers and data centers — the backbone of the cloud
ch6_nav_prev: ← Back to Linux and Open Source
